questions.text: Which of the given motion is a type of 2D motion?

questions.text: When a stone is thrown horizontally with 2 m/s from a building of height 5 m then just before hitting the ground its acceleration is:

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The acceleration of the stone just before hitting the ground will not be 12 m/s². It is not possible for the acceleration to be higher than the acceleration due to gravity, which is approximately 9.8 m/s².

questions.text: At every point of the trajectory of the projectile, which of the following quantities is always zero?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Projectile motion can be analyzed by breaking it down into horizontal and vertical components. In the absence of air resistance, the horizontal velocity of a projectile remains constant throughout its trajectory, and there is no horizontal acceleration.The vertical motion of a projectile is affected by gravity, so there is a constant vertical acceleration throughout the motion. However, at the highest point of the trajectory, the vertical velocity of the projectile becomes zero before changing direction and starting to move downward. Therefore, neither the vertical velocity nor the total velocity is always zero throughout the trajectory.Thus, the only quantity that is always zero throughout the projectile's trajectory is the horizontal acceleration.

Therefore, neither the vertical velocity nor the total velocity is always zero throughout the trajectory.Thus, the only quantity that is always zero throughout the projectile's trajectory is the horizontal acceleration.

However, at the highest point of the trajectory, the vertical velocity of the projectile becomes zero before changing direction and starting to move downward. Therefore, neither the vertical velocity nor the total velocity is always zero throughout the trajectory.Thus, the only quantity that is always zero throughout the projectile's trajectory is the horizontal acceleration.

questions.explanation: Projectile motion can be analyzed by breaking it down into horizontal and vertical components. The vertical motion of a projectile is affected by gravity, so there is a constant vertical acceleration throughout the motion. However, at the highest point of the trajectory, the vertical velocity of the projectile becomes zero before changing direction and starting to move downward. Therefore, neither the vertical velocity nor the total velocity is always zero throughout the trajectory. Thus, the only quantity that is always zero throughout the projectile's trajectory is the horizontal acceleration.

questions.text: A projectile is launched with vertical kinetic energy, K, at an angle of 8. Its variation with kinetic energy is?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The correct answer is "Parabolic." When a projectile is launched with an initial kinetic energy of K at an angle of 8°, its trajectory follows a parabolic path. This is known as projectile motion, and it is a result of the combination of the projectile's initial velocity and the gravitational force acting on it.
Projectile motion is not a periodic motion because it does not repeat at regular intervals.
A hyperbolic trajectory does not apply to a projectile launched with a finite initial kinetic energy and angle.
A linear trajectory would imply that the projectile's path is a straight line. However, in projectile motion, the trajectory is curved due to the gravitational force acting on the projectile.

questions.explanation: Projectile motion is parabolic because the vertical position of the object is influenced only by a constant acceleration and also because horizontal velocity is generally constant. Put simply, basic projectile motion is parabolic because of its related equation of motion.

questions.text: If a projectile is launched with 3m/s velocity at 60-degree angle then at the highest point its horizontal velocity is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At the highest point of the projectile's trajectory, its horizontal velocity will be equal to the initial horizontal velocity. Therefore, the horizontal velocity of the projectile at the highest point will be:v*cosθ = 3*cos(60) = 1.5 m/swhere v is the initial velocity and theta is the launch angle.

At the highest point of the projectile's trajectory, its horizontal velocity will be equal to the initial horizontal velocity. Therefore, the horizontal velocity of the projectile at the highest point will be:v*cosθ = 3*cos(60) = 1.5 m/swhere v is the initial velocity and theta is the launch angle.

Therefore, the horizontal velocity of the projectile at the highest point will be:v*cosθ = 3*cos(60) = 1.5 m/swhere v is the initial velocity and theta is the launch angle.

questions.text: A projectile is fired horizontally with an initial speed of 20 m/s, Its horizontal speed 3s later is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The horizontal speed of a projectile remains constant if air resistance is negligible.
This value is unrealistic for typical projectile speeds and does not relate to the constant horizontal velocity.
This value implies an increase in speed, which does not occur in horizontal motion without external forces.
This value might seem plausible due to gravitational acceleration, but gravity only affects vertical motion.

questions.explanation: The key principle in projectile motion is that the horizontal component of the velocity remains constant if there is no air resistance. Therefore, the horizontal speed of 20 m/s will remain unchanged 3 seconds later. Option B (6067 m/s) and Option C (60 m/s) represent incorrect interpretations of projectile motion as they suggest a change in horizontal speed. Option D (29.4 m/s) incorrectly applies gravitational acceleration to horizontal motion, which only affects vertical speed.

questions.text: A body is projected horizontally from the top of a cliff with a velocity of 9.8m/s. What time elapses before horizontal and vertical velocities become equal? Take g = 9.8m/s2
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it confuses the time with the initial horizontal velocity value. The time when velocities become equal is not 9.8 seconds.
This option is incorrect because initially, the vertical velocity is zero, and it takes some time for it to increase and become equal to the horizontal velocity.
This option is incorrect because it overestimates the time. The calculation shows that the velocities become equal at a much earlier time.
This is the correct option. At t = 1 second, the vertical velocity due to gravity becomes equal to the initial horizontal velocity.

questions.explanation: To solve the problem, we use the following steps:1. The horizontal velocity (Vx) remains constant at 9.8m/s, as there is no acceleration in the horizontal direction.2. The vertical velocity (Vy) can be calculated using the formula Vy = gt, where g = 9.8m/s2.3. We set the horizontal velocity equal to the vertical velocity: Vx = Vy = 9.8m/s.4. Substitute the values: 9.8 = 9.8t, solving for t gives t = 1s.Therefore, the time elapsed before the horizontal and vertical velocities become equal is 1 second.The other options are incorrect because they either confuse the initial velocity with time or miscalculate the time taken for the velocities to become equal.

options.explanation: At the top of the trajectory, the velocity of the projectile is zero but the acceleration is still directed downward and is equal to the acceleration due to gravity that is 9.8 m/s2.
At the top of the trajectory, the velocity of the projectile is zero but the vertical acceleration is not zero.
At the top of the trajectory, the velocity of the projectile is zero but the acceleration is still directed downward and is equal to the acceleration due to gravity that is 9.8 m/s2.
At the top of the trajectory, the velocity of the projectile is zero but the acceleration is still directed downward and is equal to the acceleration due to gravity that is 9.8 m/s2.

questions.explanation: At the top of the trajectory, the velocity of the projectile is zero but the acceleration is still directed downward and is equal to the acceleration due to gravity that is 9.8 m/s2.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at point O and follows the path OPQRS, as shown, Air resistance may be neglected. Which statement is true for the projectile when it is at the highest point Q of its path?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectiles acceleration is zero
options.explanation: This is true. The horizontal component of acceleration is zero at heighest point during projectile motion, and throughout the motion.

options.text: The kinetic energy of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Kinetic energy is not zero at the highest point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is Vx + Vy = V. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as Ucosθ, V can not be zero. Hence, kinetic energy can not be zero.

options.text: The horizontal component of the projectile's velocity is zero
options.explanation: This statement is erroneous. The horizontal velocity will remain conserved througout the journey as Ucosθ.

options.text: The momentum of the projectile is zero
options.explanation: Momentum is not zero at any point during projectile motion. The velocity of the projectile at any point is vx + vy = v. Since the horizontal velocity will remain conserved throughout the journey as vcosθ, v can not be zero. Hence, momentum can not be zero.

questions.explanation: The horizontal component of the projectile's acceleration is zero at point Q.

questions.text: A bullet is fired horizontally from a rifle at a distant target. Ignoring the effect of air resistance, what is the horizontal and vertical acceleration of the bullet?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because horizontal acceleration in projectile motion, ignoring air resistance, is zero. Vertical acceleration is due to gravity, which is 9.8 ms-2.
This is the correct answer. In projectile motion, there is no horizontal acceleration when air resistance is ignored, and the vertical acceleration is due to gravity, which is 9.8 ms-2.
This option is incorrect. Horizontal acceleration is zero in the absence of air resistance, but vertical acceleration is due to gravity, which is 9.8 ms-2.
This option is incorrect because while there is no horizontal acceleration, the vertical acceleration is not zero; it is 9.8 ms-2 due to gravity.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, when a bullet is fired horizontally, the only force acting on it after being fired is gravity, which causes a vertical acceleration of 9.8 ms-2. There is no horizontal acceleration because, in the absence of air resistance, no force acts in the horizontal direction after the bullet leaves the rifle. Therefore, the correct answer is Option B: Horizontal, 0 Vertical, 9.8 ms-2. Options A and C incorrectly assign horizontal acceleration, and Option D incorrectly suggests zero vertical acceleration.

questions.text: The P.E gained by projectile when it reaches at maximum height is expressed by equation;
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Cosine is related to the horizontal component of velocity, not the vertical height where potential energy is maximized.
Tangent is a ratio of vertical to horizontal components, not directly related to the potential energy at maximum height.

The potential energy (PE) of a projectile at its maximum height is equal to its kinetic energy (KE) at the point of projection. This is because the projectile has no vertical velocity at its maximum height, so all of its energy is in the form of potential energy. The equation for PE is: PE = mgh where: m is the mass of the projectile g is the acceleration due to gravity h is the height of the projectile The equation for KE is: KE = 1/2mv² where: m is the mass of the projectile v is the velocity of the projectile At the point of projection, the velocity of the projectile is v = vo, where vo is the initial velocity of the projectile. Substituting these equations into the equation for PE, we get: PE = mgh = 1/2mvo² Solving for h, we get: h = 1/2vo²/g The maximum height of the projectile is reached when the vertical component of its velocity is zero. This happens when the projectile has reached its maximum height and is momentarily at rest. The vertical component of the velocity of the projectile is given by: v = vo sin θ where: θ is the angle of projection Setting v = 0 and solving for θ, we get: θ = sin⁻¹(0/vo) The angle of projection at which the projectile reaches its maximum height is always 90 degrees. Therefore, the PE gained by the projectile when it reaches its maximum height is: PE = 1/2vo²/g This is equal to K.E. sin²θ, where: KE is the initial kinetic energy of the projectile θ is the angle of projection Therefore, the answer is true.
Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent and is not directly related to potential energy or height.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. At maximum height, the projectile's vertical velocity is zero, and its potential energy (PE) is maximized. The potential energy gained is equal to the initial kinetic energy (K.E.) associated with the vertical component of the initial velocity, which is given by K.E. sin²θ. This is because the vertical component of the initial velocity is vo sin θ, and the energy associated with this component is transferred into potential energy at the peak. Option A is incorrect because cosine pertains to horizontal motion. Option B is incorrect because tangent refers to a ratio of vertical to horizontal components, not directly to potential energy. Option D is incorrect as cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent and does not relate to maximum height or potential energy.

questions.text: At which point for a projectile its kinetic energy is completely converted into potential energy?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At the point of projection, the projectile has maximum kinetic energy and minimum potential energy. Thus, kinetic energy is not converted into potential energy at this point.
While the vertical component of velocity is zero at the highest point, the horizontal component still exists. Therefore, kinetic energy is reduced but not entirely converted into potential energy.

Upon hitting the ground, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the height is zero, and kinetic energy is maximum due to maximum speed. Thus, kinetic energy is not converted into potential energy at this point.
In projectile motion, kinetic energy is never completely converted into potential energy. Even at the highest point, there is always some kinetic energy due to the horizontal component of velocity.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the kinetic energy is never fully converted into potential energy due to the constant horizontal velocity component. At the highest point, the projectile has minimum kinetic energy, but not zero, as the horizontal motion continues. Therefore, the correct answer is that it is not possible for kinetic energy to be completely converted into potential energy.

questions.text: A body is thrown with a velocity of 9.8m/s making an angle of 30o with the horizontal. It will hit the ground after a time:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct because the formula for the time of flight is 2(vi sin θ)/g. By substituting the given values: 2 x 9.8 x sin(30°) / 9.8, it simplifies to 1 second.
This is incorrect. Using the formula, the calculated time of flight does not equal 2 seconds.
This is incorrect. The time of flight calculated from the formula is different from 3 seconds.
This is incorrect. The result from the formula does not match 1.5 seconds.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. The formula for the time of flight of a projectile launched at an angle θ with an initial velocity vi is given by 2(vi sin θ)/g, where g is the acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.8 m/s2). Substituting the values provided (vi = 9.8 m/s and θ = 30°), we get: 2 x 9.8 x sin(30°) / 9.8 = 1 second. The other options (B, C, and D) are incorrect because they do not result from this calculation and are not consistent with the physics of projectile motion.

questions.text: In projectile motion the
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Acceleration is parallel (or antiparallel) to the velocity.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because in projectile motion, only the vertical component of motion is accelerated, while the horizontal component remains constant.

options.text: Acceleration is vertical, while the velocity can be in any direction.
options.explanation: This is correct. In projectile motion, gravitational acceleration acts only in the vertical direction, while the velocity vector can have both horizontal and vertical components.

options.text: Acceleration is perpendicular to the velocity.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. In projectile motion, the acceleration due to gravity is vertical, not perpendicular to the velocity, which can have both horizontal and vertical components.

options.text: Acceleration is vertical and the velocity is horizontal.
options.explanation: This is partially correct, but misleading. While the acceleration is indeed vertical, the velocity can have a vertical component as well, especially at the beginning or end of the trajectory.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the only acceleration acting on the object is due to gravity, which acts in the vertical direction. Therefore, the correct statement is that the acceleration is vertical, while the velocity can have both horizontal and vertical components. Option B correctly captures this concept. Option A is incorrect because it suggests that acceleration is always parallel or antiparallel to velocity, which is not true for projectile motion. Option C incorrectly states that acceleration is perpendicular to velocity, which is not a general rule for projectile motion. Option D is misleading because it suggests that velocity only has a horizontal component, ignoring the fact that there can be a vertical component of velocity as well.

questions.text: A projectile is fired horizontally with an initial speed of 20 m/s. Its horizontal speed 3 s later will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option correctly states that the horizontal speed of the projectile remains constant at 20 m/s. In horizontal projectile motion, there is no horizontal acceleration, so the initial horizontal speed remains unchanged unless an external force acts on it.
This option incorrectly suggests a decrease in speed. In horizontal projectile motion, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant since there is no horizontal acceleration.
This option incorrectly suggests an increase in speed. The horizontal component of velocity remains constant in horizontal projectile motion unless an external force acts on the projectile.
This option incorrectly proposes an increase in horizontal speed. In horizontal projectile motion, the horizontal speed remains constant at 20 m/s due to the absence of horizontal acceleration.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, particularly when a projectile is fired horizontally, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the flight because there is no horizontal acceleration acting on the projectile. The only acceleration in projectile motion is the vertical acceleration due to gravity, which affects only the vertical component of velocity. Therefore, the horizontal speed of the projectile after 3 seconds remains 20 m/s.Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they suggest changes in the horizontal speed, which contradicts the principle of constant horizontal velocity in projectile motion.

questions.text: A ball is thrown up with 20 ms-1 at an angle of 60° with x-axis. The horizontal velocity of the ball at the top position is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At the top of the trajectory, only the vertical component of velocity is zero, not the horizontal component. Hence, this option is incorrect.
This represents the initial velocity of the ball, not the horizontal component at the top. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The horizontal component of velocity, Vx = Vcosθ = (20)cos60° = (20)(0.5) = 10 ms-1. This is the correct answer.
This value does not correspond to any standard calculation for the components of velocity in this context. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the motion, as there is no horizontal acceleration (ignoring air resistance). The formula for the horizontal component of velocity is Vx = Vcosθ. Given that V = 20 ms-1 and θ = 60°, we calculate Vx = 20cos60° = 20 × 0.5 = 10 ms-1. Therefore, the correct answer is 10 ms-1.Option A incorrectly assumes the horizontal velocity is zero, which applies only to the vertical component at the peak. Option B incorrectly suggests the initial velocity is the same as the horizontal component. Option D provides an arbitrary value that does not result from the given calculations.

questions.text: A bomb is dropped from an aeroplane moving horizontally with a speed of 200 mph (miles per hour). If the air resistance is negligible, the bomb will reach the ground in 5 sec when the altitude is (g = 10ms-2):
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The calculation should be in meters, not miles. The formula d = 1/2 * g * t^2 gives the vertical distance in meters, resulting in 125 m.
This option is incorrect. Using the formula d = 1/2 * g * t^2, the calculated vertical distance is 125 m, not 40 m.
This is the correct option. The vertical distance is calculated using d = 1/2 * g * t^2 = 1/2 * 10 m/s^2 * 5^2 s^2, which equals 125 m.
This option is incorrect. The correct calculation using d = 1/2 * g * t^2 results in a vertical distance of 125 m, not 10 m.

questions.explanation: The bomb is released from an aeroplane moving horizontally, but its vertical motion is influenced solely by gravity. The horizontal speed of 200 mph does not affect the time taken to hit the ground. The formula for vertical distance under gravity is d = 1/2 * g * t^2. Here, g = 10 m/s² and t = 5 s, so the distance d = 1/2 * 10 * 5² = 125 m.Option A is incorrect because it uses the wrong unit (miles instead of meters). Option B also underestimates the distance, while Option D severely underestimates it. Only Option C correctly identifies the vertical distance as 125 m.

questions.text: What is the acceleration of a projectile at its highest point:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The acceleration is not maximum at the highest point; it remains constant due to gravity.
The acceleration is not zero at the highest point; the acceleration due to gravity is always acting on the projectile.
The acceleration does not vary; it remains constant throughout the motion due to gravity.
The acceleration of a projectile at its highest point is g, the gravitational acceleration, which is approximately 9.8 m/s2 downward.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the only force acting is gravity, which causes a constant downward acceleration of g (approximately 9.8 m/s2). This remains true throughout the projectile's flight, including at the highest point. At the highest point, the vertical component of velocity is zero, but the acceleration due to gravity is still present, pulling the projectile downward. Therefore, the acceleration is not zero, maximum, or minimum; it is consistently g. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they suggest a variation or absence of acceleration, which contradicts the constant presence of gravitational acceleration.

questions.text: Identical guns fire identical bullets horizontally at the same speed from the same height above level planes, one on the Earth and one on the Moon. Which of the following three statements is/are true?I. The horizontal distance traveled by the bullet is greater for the Moon.II. The flight time is less for the bullet on the Earth.III. The velocity of the bullets at impact are the same.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The velocity of the bullets at impact are not the same because the gravitational acceleration is different on Earth and the Moon, affecting their vertical velocities at impact.
Statement III is incorrect due to different gravitational accelerations, so this option is incorrect. Statement I is correct, as lesser gravity on the Moon results in a longer horizontal distance.
Both statements I and II are correct. The bullet on the Moon travels a greater horizontal distance due to lower gravity, and the flight time is less on Earth due to its higher gravitational pull.

Statement III is incorrect because different gravitational forces cause different impact velocities. Statement II is correct because the bullet on Earth has a shorter flight time due to higher gravity.

questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on the Moon is lower than on Earth. As a result, the bullet fired on the Moon takes longer to fall to the ground, leading to a greater horizontal distance traveled (Statement I is true). The flight time is shorter on Earth due to stronger gravity pulling the bullet down faster, making Statement II true. The velocities at impact differ because the vertical component of velocity, which depends on gravity, will be higher on Earth, making Statement III false.

questions.text: A stone is thrown horizontally with an initial speed 30 m/s from a bridge. Find the stone's total speed when it enters the water4 s later. (Ignore air resistance.)
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the initial horizontal speed of the stone and does not account for the increase in velocity due to gravity.
This is the vertical speed after 4 seconds due to gravity alone, ignoring the initial horizontal speed.

vi = vh = 30 m/s {as the stone is thrown horizontally} initial vertical component of the velocity = vv = 0 t = 4 s final vertical component of velocity: vf = vi + at vf = 0+ 10(4) = 40 m/s total final velocity2 = vh2 + vv2 total final velocity2 =302 + 402 total final velocity2 = 2500 total final velocity = √2500 = 50 m/s
This speed is greater than the actual resultant speed and is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The stone is thrown with an initial horizontal velocity of 30 m/s, so the horizontal component of its velocity remains constant at 30 m/s because there is no horizontal acceleration. The vertical component of velocity can be calculated using the equation vf = vi + at, where vi is the initial vertical velocity (0 m/s), a is the acceleration due to gravity (10 m/s2), and t is the time (4 s). Therefore, the vertical velocity after 4 seconds is vf = 0 + 10(4) = 40 m/s. The total speed of the stone is the resultant velocity, which is the vector sum of the horizontal and vertical components: √(302 + 402) = √2500 = 50 m/s.Options A and B are incorrect because they represent only one component of the motion. Option D is incorrect because it overestimates the resultant speed.

questions.text: In projectile motion the acceleration in the vertical direction is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The vertical acceleration in projectile motion is solely due to gravity, which is constant at approximately 9.8 m/s2 downwards. This makes this option correct.
In projectile motion, the only vertical force is gravity, which is constant. Therefore, the vertical acceleration does not change with time, making this option incorrect.
There is a constant gravitational pull acting on the projectile vertically, meaning the vertical acceleration cannot be zero. Thus, this option is incorrect.
The direction of vertical acceleration is typically considered downwards, which is usually taken as negative in conventional coordinate systems. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In projectile motion, the only force acting on the projectile in the vertical direction is gravity. This results in a constant vertical acceleration of approximately 9.8 m/s2 downwards. Hence, the correct answer is that the vertical acceleration remains constant. The other options are incorrect because: the acceleration does not vary with time, it is not zero because gravity acts continuously, and it is conventionally considered negative, not positive, in standard coordinate systems.

questions.text: Which of the curves on the graph below best represents the vertical component "vy" of the velocity versus the time t for a projectile fired at an angle of 45o above the horizontal?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The gradient of the velocity-time graph shows acceleration. The vertical component of acceleration is constant throughout the motion, so, the gradient must also be constant. Whereas the line OE is curved. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The gradient of the velocity-time graph shows acceleration. The vertical component of acceleration is constant throughout the motion so, the gradient must also be constant. Whereas the gradient of line AB is 0. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The gradient of the velocity-time graph shows acceleration. The vertical component of acceleration is constant throughout the motion so, the gradient must also be constant. Whereas the gradient of line DE is 0. Hence, this option is incorrect.

During a projectile motion the vertical component of velocity, changes with time, and is zero at the maximum height. Since line AE shows zero velocity at the midpoint where the maximum height is attained this line is correct. The velocity first decreases then it increases (in opposite direction), and the acceleration is constant throughout, producing a straight line of negative gradient.

questions.explanation: During a projectile motion the vertical component of velocity, changes with time, and is zero at the maximum height. Since line AE shows zero velocity at the midpoint where the maximum height is attained this line is correct. The velocity first decreases then it increases (in the opposite direction), and the acceleration is constant throughout, producing a straight line of negative gradient. The figure below shows the position-time graph for the projectile. The "OP" portion represents the "AH" portion of the graph and "PA" represents "HE", with zero velocity at point H which is the maximum height point P.

questions.text: At maximum height on the trajectory which component of projectile becomes zero;
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is always acting downwards, even at the maximum height. Therefore, it does not become zero at any point in the projectile's motion.

While the overall velocity is not zero at maximum height, it's important to note that only the vertical component of the velocity becomes zero at this point. The horizontal component remains unchanged.
This is the correct answer. At the maximum height, the vertical component of the projectile's velocity becomes zero, as it momentarily stops rising and is about to start descending.

The horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the projectile's flight because there are no horizontal forces acting on it. Thus, it does not become zero at the maximum height or at any other point.

questions.explanation: At the maximum height of a projectile's trajectory, the vertical component of its velocity becomes zero. This is because the projectile has reached the peak of its path and is about to change direction from upward to downward. The acceleration due to gravity continues to act, pulling the projectile downward, but it does not affect the horizontal component of the velocity, which remains constant. Therefore, the correct answer is Option C: Vertical velocity. The other options are incorrect because acceleration continues to act due to gravity, the overall velocity isn't zero, it's just the vertical component that becomes zero, and the horizontal velocity remains unchanged throughout the motion.

questions.text: A projectile is fired horizontally with an initial speed of 20 m/s, Its horizontal speed 3s later is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The horizontal speed of a projectile remains constant if air resistance is negligible.
This value is unrealistic for typical projectile speeds and does not relate to the constant horizontal velocity.
This value implies an increase in speed, which does not occur in horizontal motion without external forces.
This value might seem plausible due to gravitational acceleration, but gravity only affects vertical motion.

questions.explanation: The key principle in projectile motion is that the horizontal component of the velocity remains constant if there is no air resistance. Therefore, the horizontal speed of 20 m/s will remain unchanged 3 seconds later. Option B (6067 m/s) and Option C (60 m/s) represent incorrect interpretations of projectile motion as they suggest a change in horizontal speed. Option D (29.4 m/s) incorrectly applies gravitational acceleration to horizontal motion, which only affects vertical speed.

questions.text: To throw a stone horizontally from the vertical height of 10m as it covers the horizontal distance of 10m find the x—component of velocity (g = 10 ms-2):
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This velocity is too low. It suggests a miscalculation in either the time of flight or the horizontal velocity calculation.
This velocity is still underestimating the required horizontal component to cover the given distance.
This is the correct velocity. Using the time of flight derived from the vertical motion, the horizontal velocity calculation matches the required distance.
This velocity overestimates the horizontal component, indicating an error in the calculation of time or distance.

questions.explanation: To solve this problem, we first need to determine the time it takes for the stone to fall 10 meters vertically. Using the equation of motion for vertical displacement, S = Ut + 1/2 at2, where U (initial vertical velocity) is 0, we have:10 = 0 + 1/2 * 10 * t210 = 5t2t2 = 2t = √2 secondsNow, using the time of flight in the horizontal motion equation, Speed = distance/time, we calculate:Speed = 10/√2Speed ≈ 7.1 ms-1The correct answer is 7.1 ms-1, as it accurately represents the horizontal velocity needed to cover 10 meters in the time it takes to fall 10 meters vertically.Other options either underestimate or overestimate the required velocity due to miscalculations in time or distance.

questions.text: Which shows the correct relation between time of flight T and maximum height H?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct relationship between time of flight T and maximum height H in projectile motion.
This equation incorrectly suggests that maximum height H decreases with increasing time of flight T, which is not accurate according to the laws of projectile motion.
This equation incorrectly proposes that the maximum height H is directly proportional to T² while also having an inversion with g, which does not reflect the correct physics of projectile motion.
This expression incorrectly implies that maximum height H is inversely related to both g and T², which contradicts the correct relationship.
This equation is incorrect as it does not represent the proper relationship between time of flight and maximum height for projectile motion.

questions.explanation: The correct option is H = gT²/8, which derives from the equations of motion for a projectile under the influence of gravity. In this formula, g represents the acceleration due to gravity, and T is the total time of flight. The formula indicates that the maximum height achieved by a projectile is directly proportional to the square of the time of flight divided by a factor that incorporates gravitational acceleration.All other options are incorrect due to misrepresenting the relationships dictated by the equations of motion. Options B, C, D, and E fail to follow the correct physical laws governing projectile motion, leading to erroneous conclusions about how height relates to time of flight.

questions.text: The path followed by a projectile is known as its:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Range refers to the horizontal distance covered by a projectile from its launch point to where it lands. It does not describe the shape or full path of the projectile's motion.

Trajectory is the correct term for the path a projectile follows through space. It includes both the horizontal and vertical components of motion and is influenced by gravity and other forces. Typically, in the absence of air resistance, this path is a parabola.
A cycle usually refers to a complete sequence of events in periodic motion, not the path of a projectile. It is unrelated to projectile motion.

Height describes the vertical distance above a reference point but does not define the full path of a projectile. It is particularly used to refer to the maximum vertical distance reached by the projectile.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Trajectory, as it specifically describes the path followed by a projectile in motion, considering both its horizontal and vertical components. It accounts for the influence of gravity and any other forces acting on the projectile, resulting in a parabolic path under ideal conditions.Option A, Range, only covers the horizontal distance and not the full path. Option C, Cycle, refers to repetitive sequences, unrelated to projectile motion. Option D, Height, describes only the vertical aspect and not the entire path of motion.

questions.text: A particle is projected from the ground with a kinetic energy E at an angle of 60 degrees with the horizontal. Its kinetic energy at the highest point of its motion will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Kinetic energy will not remain constant as velocity of the particle changes.
As K.E is directly proportional to the square of velocity, the K.E of the body should be ¼th of the original value as velocity is reduced to half.

At the highest point, the vertical component of velocity will be zero. The horizontal component Vx is given by, Vx= V cosθ Vx = V cos 60= V/2 The kinetic energy will be given by: K.E = ½ mv2 K.E’ = ½ m(V/2)2 K.E’ = ½ m(v2/4) K.E’ = ¼(K.E)

Incorrect based on calculations. At the highest point, the vertical component of velocity will be zero. The horizontal component Vx is given by , Vx= V cosθ Vx = V cos 60= V/2 The kinetic energy will be given by: K.E = ½ mv2 K.E’ = ½ m(V/2)2 K.E’ = ½ m(v2/4) K.E’ = ¼(K.E)

questions.explanation: At the highest point, the vertical component of velocity will be zero. The horizontal component Vx is given by , Vx= V cosθ Vx = V cos 60= V/2 The kinetic energy will be given by: K.E = ½ mv2 K.E’ = ½ m(V/2)2 K.E’ = ½ m(v2/4) K.E’ = ¼(K.E)

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is true concerning the motion of an ideal projectile launched at an angle of 45° to the horizontal?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The acceleration vector points opposite to the velocity vector on the way up and in the same direction as the velocity vector on the way down.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In projectile motion, the acceleration due to gravity always acts downward, regardless of whether the object is moving up or down. Therefore, the acceleration vector is always opposite to the velocity vector.

options.text: The speed at the top of the trajectory is zero.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The speed at the top of the trajectory is not zero; it is the component of the initial velocity in the vertical direction. The horizontal component of the velocity remains constant throughout the entire motion.

options.text: The object's total speed remains constant during the entire flight.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The object's total speed, which is the magnitude of its velocity vector, changes during the flight. The horizontal component of the velocity remains constant, but the vertical component changes due to the effect of gravity.

options.text: The horizontal speed decreases on the way up and also decreases on the way down.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In ideal projectile motion, neglecting air resistance, the horizontal speed remains constant throughout the entire motion. No horizontal forces are acting on the projectile, so there is no change in its horizontal speed.

options.text: The vertical speed decreases on the way up and increases on the way down.

questions.explanation: The only external force acting on the object is the gravitational force vertically downwards. Thus the acceleration of the projectile is g downwards always. Thus there is no change in the speed of the object in the horizontal direction. The speed of an object in the vertical direction at the top of the trajectory is zero. However, the net speed at the point is equal to the constant horizontal component. Due to downward constant acceleration, the vertical speed decreases way up, becomes zero, and then increases way down. Thus option E is correct.

questions.text: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 2 m and with a uniform speed of 5 ms–1. What will be the change in velocity when the particle completes half of the revolution?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect because there is a change in velocity when the particle completes half of the revolution.
Correct. The change in velocity is 10 ms–1 when the particle completes half of the revolution.
Incorrect because the change in velocity is not doubled based on the particle's path and speed.
Incorrect because the change in velocity is greater due to the circular path and speed of the particle.

questions.explanation: When the particle completes half of the revolution, it covers 180 degrees. The change in velocity is calculated as follows: △v = 5 ms–1 - (-5 ms–1) = 10 ms–1. This change in velocity is due to the change in direction along the circular path despite the uniform speed of the particle. Therefore, option B is the correct answer.

options.text: Change in angular rotation / change in time

options.text: Change in speed / change in time
options.explanation: Change in speed/change in time is linear acceleration.

options.text: Change in acceleration / change in time
options.explanation: Incorrect Relation.

questions.explanation: Angular velocity is the time rate at which an object rotates or revolves about an axis. Angular velocity is represented by the Greek letter omega (ω, sometimes Ω). It is measured in angle per unit time; hence, the SI unit of angular velocity is radians per second. Also;Angular velocity is the vector measure of the rotation rate, which refers to how fast an object rotates or revolves relative to another point. In simple words, angular velocity is the time rate at which an object rotates or revolves about an axis.

questions.text: The force responsible for the circular motion of the electron around the nucleus is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Gravitational force is much weaker compared to the Coulomb force and is not significant at the atomic scale.
The frictional force is a macroscopic force that arises due to the relative motion or tendency of motion between two objects. It is not applicable to the microscopic scale of atomic interactions.

The force responsible for the circular motion of the electron around the nucleus is the Coulomb force. This force is the electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and the negatively charged electrons. According to the laws of electromagnetism, opposite charges attract each other, and the force between them is given by Coulomb's Law. In the case of an atom, the Coulomb force acts as a centripetal force, keeping the electrons in orbit around the nucleus.

Cohesive forces are typically associated with interactions between molecules of the same substance. While cohesive forces exist, they are not the primary force responsible for the circular motion of electrons around the nucleus.

questions.explanation: The force responsible for the circular motion of the electron around the nucleus is the Coulomb force. This force is the electrostatic force of attraction between the positively charged nucleus and the negatively charged electrons. According to the laws of electromagnetism, opposite charges attract each other, and the force between them is given by Coulomb's Law. In the case of an atom, the Coulomb force acts as a centripetal force, keeping the electrons in orbit around the nucleus.

questions.text: An object thrown in arbitrary direction, in space with an initial velocity and moving freely under gravity will follow:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When an object is thrown in an arbitrary direction in space with an initial velocity and is moving freely under gravity, its trajectory is a parabola. The force acting on the object is the gravitational force, and the object's motion can be described by the equations of motion, which result in a parabolic path.

questions.text: The velocity of a projectile is maximum:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The velocity of a projectile is maximum at two points in its trajectory: at the point of projection and just before striking the ground. At the point of projection, the initial velocity is entirely in the horizontal direction, so the horizontal component of velocity is maximum. Just before striking the ground, the projectile is moving vertically with the highest speed due to the acceleration by gravity. The horizontal component of velocity remains constant (ignoring air resistance), but the vertical component increases, reaching its maximum just before hitting the ground.

questions.text: The angle subtended at the center of a circle by an arc of length equal to its radius is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In a circle, there are 2 π radians in one full rotation (360 degrees). The length of the arc formed by an angle in radians is given by the formula s=rθ, where s is the arc length, r is the radius, and θ is the angle in radians. If the length of the arc is equal to the radius ( s = r ), then the angle (θ) in radians is also equal to 1 radian. So, an angle subtended at the center of a circle by an arc of length equal to its radius is equal to one radian.

questions.text: The direction of the linear velocity of a body at a point moving along a circular path is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The linear velocity of a body moving along a circular path is directed along the tangent to the circle at the point where the body is located. This is because the velocity vector at any point on a circular path is perpendicular to the radius vector at that point. Therefore, the correct direction of the linear velocity is along the tangent to the circle at the given point.
This option is incorrect because the linear velocity is not directed along the axis of rotation in the case of circular motion.

This is because the velocity vector at any point on a circular path is perpendicular to the radius vector at that point. Therefore, the correct direction of the linear velocity is along the tangent to the circle at the given point.

questions.text: A body is moving with a constant speed v in a circle of radius r. Its angular acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Refer to the explanation given below.
This option is incorrect. Refer to the explanation given below.
This option is correct. Refer to the explanation given below.
This option is incorrect. Refer to the explanation given below.

questions.explanation: When a body is moving with a constant speed v in a circle of radius r, it is undergoing uniform circular motion. In uniform circular motion, the speed of the object is constant, but its direction is continuously changing. The angular acceleration (α) is defined as the rate of change of angular velocity (ω) with respect to time (t). The formula for angular acceleration (α) is given by: α = Δω/Δt In uniform circular motion, the angular velocity is constant because the speed is constant. Therefore, there is no change in angular velocity, and Δω is zero. As a result, the angular acceleration (α) is zero.

questions.text: On a railway curve, the outside rail is laid higher than the inside one so that resultant force exerted on the wheels of the rail car by the tops of the rail will:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a train or railcar goes around a curve, it experiences a centripetal force directed towards the center of the curve. This centripetal force is required to keep the train moving along the curved track. To achieve this, the outer rail is laid higher than the inner rail.

questions.explanation: The explanation is given below: When a train or railcar goes around a curve, it experiences a centripetal force directed towards the center of the curve. This centripetal force is required to keep the train moving along the curved track. To achieve this, the outer rail is laid higher than the inner rail. The elevated outer rail helps in two ways: Compensating for the tendency to overturn: When a train moves around a curve, there is a natural tendency for it to want to move outward (away from the center of the curve) due to inertia. By elevating the outer rail, the resultant force on the wheels has a horizontal component that acts inward, counteracting the outward tendency and preventing the train from derailing. Balancing the centripetal force: The elevated outer rail helps balance the centripetal force acting on the train. This force is necessary to keep the train moving in a curved path. By tilting the track, the gravitational force acting on the train has a component that also contributes to the centripetal force, helping to keep the train on the track.
hints: laid higher than the inside one
